summary: I was in Las Vegas in early March 2020 attending a special event trade show when the COVID-19 pandemic started to unfold. The city began shutting down while I was there, and it was a scary and uncertain time. I remember the shows and some hotels closing down, and I had to quickly make my way back home as the situation escalated.

I first realized how serious the pandemic was when my husband called me from home to say that the National Basketball Association was shutting down. That was a clear signal that things were not normal and that the pandemic was going to impact everything. Hearing that the NBA was stopping play was one of the first big signs that made me start to prepare for what was coming.

When I returned to St. Louis after the trade show in Vegas, I immediately started getting messages from clients canceling events because the city was shutting down. St. Louis went from allowing 100 people to 50, then 20, and eventually no events at all. It was a frightening time because my business was heavily booked for 2020, and suddenly everything stopped.

During the early days of the pandemic, I was glued to CNN to stay informed about what was happening. Watching the news helped me understand the severity of the COVID-19 pandemic and the government responses. CNN was one of the main sources I relied on to learn about available support for small businesses and updates on the health crisis.

Along with CNN, I also watched CNBC a lot during the pandemic to keep up with the economic impact and financial news. CNBC provided important information about loans, grants, and financial aid programs that helped me apply for support to keep my business afloat. It was a key resource for understanding how to navigate the economic challenges of 2020.

The COVID-19 pandemic completely changed my life and business starting in March 2020. I was in Las Vegas when it began, and soon after, everything shut down in St. Louis, including events and hotels. We had to pivot our business from large conventions to smaller rentals, and my family played a big role in helping keep things going. It was a scary time filled with uncertainty, but also a period of learning and adaptation.

The year 2020 was incredibly challenging for me and my business because the pandemic caused widespread shutdowns. I had been fully booked for events, but everything was canceled, forcing us to pivot to renting equipment for smaller gatherings and press conferences. I also spent much of 2020 investing and learning from my husband, which helped us financially during the crisis.

In 2021, things were still tough, especially in May when there were fewer lifelines for small businesses like mine. Although the worst of the shutdowns were behind us, the year was marked by ongoing challenges and cautious steps toward recovery. My family and I continued to adapt, and we were more careful about social interactions, but we also started seeing some return to smaller events and gatherings.

At the beginning of 2022, we still faced difficulties as the pandemic's effects lingered, but there was a gradual return to more normal activities. My business continued to focus on smaller, private events rather than large conventions, and my family remained cautious but hopeful. The early months of 2022 were a time of ongoing adjustment and cautious optimism for the future.
